title: Dog Licking Joints Excessively: Causes, Urgency, and Triage Tips
meta_desc: Is your dog licking their joints? Learn about common causes like arthritis or allergies, and determine if your pet needs an urgent vet visit.
content_html:
TL;DR: Excessive joint licking in dogs usually signals underlying pain, allergies, or arthritis; while typically not an emergency, it requires monitoring to prevent secondary skin infections or sores.

Why is my dog excessively licking their joints?

When a dog excessively licks their joints, it is usually a physical sign of discomfort, pain, or irritation. This behavior is often their way of trying to soothe a specific area. Common underlying causes include localized skin allergies, environmental irritants, or internal joint pain caused by conditions like osteoarthritis or previous injuries. In some cases, it may also be a behavioral response to stress or boredom, though physical causes should always be ruled out first.

Is my dog's joint licking a veterinary emergency?

  • The urgency level for this concern is generally Low, as it is rarely a life-threatening emergency.
  • Monitor your dog for signs that increase urgency, such as sudden limping, visible discharge or pus, or an inability to settle.
  • If the licking results in a raw, bleeding sore known as a lick granuloma, schedule a veterinary appointment within a few days.

How can a photo of my dog's joint help with a veterinary triage?

  • A clear, high-resolution photo allows the vet to see the exact state of the skin, checking for redness, swelling, or hair loss.
  • It provides a baseline to track if the irritation is spreading or worsening over time.
  • Visual evidence helps the veterinary team provide a more accurate diagnosis and a faster path to relief for your pet.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Excessive licking of joints in dogs can be associated with joint trauma, such as cranial cruciate ligament rupture, often presenting with hindlimb lameness and anterior drawer motion of the stifles. Idiopathic polyarthritis, more common in large breeds like German Shepherds and retrievers, or toy breeds like Toy Poodles and Yorkshire Terriers, may manifest solely as arthritis with no evidence of primary infectious disease. Septic arthritis, frequently caused by bacterial agents like staphylococci and streptococci, can result from hematogenous spread. Diagnosis of underlying joint issues involves radiography for joint effusion and possible erosive bone destruction, as well as joint fluid analysis for increased cell counts.

Chapter: Orthopedics, Immunology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1198)

faqs.question: Can joint licking be a sign of arthritis?
faqs.answer: Yes, dogs often lick the skin directly over a joint to try and soothe the deep-seated ache associated with arthritis or joint inflammation.

faqs.question: Should I use a cone to stop the licking?
faqs.answer: Using an Elizabethan collar (cone) can be helpful to prevent the dog from creating a secondary skin infection while you wait for your veterinary appointment.

faqs.question: Does licking the joints mean my dog has a skin allergy?
faqs.answer: It could be a skin allergy, but because it is localized to the joint, it is equally possible that the dog is reacting to internal pain or a specific environmental irritant.
